summary: Gruber dismantles Dan Lyons's Newsweek argument that the Verizon iPhone arrived 'too late' because Android had already won over Verizon customers. He points out that Lyons's Model T analogy actually undermines his own argument, since the Model T dominated the market. Gruber argues that the vast pool of Verizon users who have never owned a smartphone makes the iPhone's arrival a massive opportunity, not a missed one. He reframes Lyons's characterization of Apple's 'control-freak nature' as a weakness, arguing it is precisely Apple's greatest strength — that's called design. Gruber cites Apple's profit leadership and 19 consecutive quarters of market share growth to counter the 'niche player' narrative, and notes Apple's worst years came when it was least controlling.
key_points: Lyons's Model T analogy backfires — the Model T dominated the US market for over a decade, undermining his argument about Apple's limited-choice approach
The huge pool of Verizon customers who have never owned a smartphone represents massive untapped demand for the iPhone, making 'too late' a dubious claim
Apple's so-called 'control-freak nature' is better described as design focus, and has driven its market success rather than hindered it
Apple leads the PC industry in profit and has grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters despite being called a 'niche player'
Apple's worst business period coincided with its least controlling era — the Performa/clone-maker years
money_quotes: There's a big difference between the iPhone being too late to get Dan Lyons's business, and being too late to rack up, say, 15 million Verizon sales by the end of 2011.
Swap in the word perfectionist and it changes the connotation.
'We're going to make these decisions for you and offer a limited number of choices' is indeed the company's philosophy. That's called design.
Apple is indeed more focused on design than its competitors. It's also been far more successful than its competitors over the past decade, in several lucrative markets.
So I'd say what Lyons calls 'Apple's big weakness' is in fact precisely the company's biggest strength.
Apple's 'niche' in the PC market today is that for profitable computers.
The worst years for Apple's PC business occurred when Apple was the least control-freakish.
I can't decide whether Lyons is really this wrong, or if The Daily Beast makes its writers post eye-rollingly contrarian stuff like this just to get links.
key_insight: What critics dismiss as Apple's 'control-freak' weakness is actually its defining strength — disciplined design focus that has driven market dominance across multiple product categories.
